answer:
Out of it — mostly.

Verdict: Don't do full event sourcing (event store, projections, replay, CQRS). Do build an append-only billing ledger. You get 80% of the benefit at 20% of the cost.

Why not full ES with a team of 4:

- Complexity tax: projections, rebuilds, eventual consistency, schema/event versioning, snapshotting. Each is a project. You'll spend months on plumbing instead of billing logic.
- Event versioning is the killer: billing rules change constantly (pricing, proration, tax). Replaying old events under new code = subtle money bugs. You'll need upcasters and versioned handlers forever.
- Eventual consistency vs. billing: customers and support expect read-your-writes on invoices. Fighting projection lag adds ops burden.
- Hiring/onboarding: ES expertise is rare; four people means bus factor ~1.
- Usage-based charges: high event volume tempts you to ES, but what you actually need is idempotent ingestion + aggregation windows — a metering pipeline, not an event-sourced domain model.

What billing actually requires — and how to get it cheaply:

- Immutability/audit: append-only tables. Never UPDATE invoices/charges; issue credit notes and adjustments. Double-entry ledger for balances.
- Point-in-time answers: the ledger gives you this without replay.
- Usage: raw usage events in append-only table (or Kafka → warehouse), idempotency keys, rate at invoice time from raw records. Recompute possible = "replay" without ES machinery.
- Corrections: explicit reversal entries, not event replay.

If you insist on ES: confine it to one bounded context (the ledger/metering core), keep subscriptions/plans as boring CRUD, use an off-the-shelf store (EventStoreDB, or Postgres append table), never replay through current business rules — store computed amounts in events.

Also seriously consider Stripe Billing/Metronome/Lago for subscriptions + metering and build only your differentiating logic. Team of 4 building billing from scratch is usually the wrong bet unless billing is the product.
